questions.question: Explain the difference between Dense Mode and Sparse Mode multicast routing protocols.
keyPoints: Dense Mode uses flood-and-prune approach suitable for densely populated groups
Sparse Mode uses explicit join messages suitable for widely distributed receivers
Dense Mode: DVMRP, PIM-DM; Sparse Mode: PIM-SM, CBT
Dense Mode has more state information, Sparse Mode scales better

questions.question: What is Reverse Path Broadcasting (RPB) and how does it prevent loops in multicast routing?
keyPoints: RPB forwards multicast packets only on interfaces leading toward receivers
Router checks if packet arrived on interface toward the source (RPF check)
If received on wrong interface, packet is dropped
This ensures each branch is reached exactly once without duplication

questions.question: Describe the role of IGMP in multicast communication.
keyPoints: IGMP is used by hosts to report multicast group memberships to local routers
Hosts send join messages to join a multicast group
Hosts send leave messages when they want to leave
Routers periodically query to discover group members on connected networks

questions.question: What is MBONE and how does tunneling work in it?
keyPoints: MBONE is the Multicast Backbone - virtual network for IP multicasting
Consists of multicast-enabled routers connected by tunnels
Tunneling encapsulates multicast packets in unicast packets
Allows multicast across networks that do not support multicast natively

questions.question: Explain the concept of Shared Tree vs Source-Specific Tree in multicast routing.
keyPoints: Shared Tree uses single tree rooted at Rendezvous Point (RP) shared by all sources
Source-Specific Tree creates separate tree from each source to receivers
Shared tree requires less router state but may have suboptimal paths
Source tree provides optimal paths but scales poorly with many sources

questions.question: How does MOSPF extend OSPF for multicast routing?
keyPoints: MOSPF introduces Group Membership LSAs to distribute group information
Routers maintain multicast group membership in their LSDB
Each router computes multicast distribution trees using SPF algorithm
Routes are computed on-demand when first multicast packet arrives

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Shared Trees in multicast routing?
keyPoints: Advantages: Less router state memory, scales well with many sources, simpler configuration
Disadvantages: Potentially suboptimal paths from source to receiver, RP can become bottleneck
All traffic must go through RP even if direct path exists
Trade-off between scalability and optimal routing

questions.question: Explain the operation of MOSPF protocol with emphasis on its components, message types, and how it builds multicast distribution trees.
keyPoints: MOSPF is extension of OSPFv2 for multicast routing
Uses Group Membership LSA (new LSA type) to advertise group membership
Routers maintain local group membership database
When traffic arrives, router uses link-state database to compute source-group SPT
Router performs RPF check to prevent loops
On-demand calculation of routes rather than maintaining per-group state
Scalability issues with many groups - uses designated routers for summarization
Comparison with other protocols like DVMRP, PIM-SM

questions.question: Discuss the challenges in multicast routing and explain how different multicast routing protocols address these challenges.
keyPoints: Challenge 1: Scalability - protocols like CBT, PIM-SM use shared trees to reduce state
Challenge 2: Loop prevention - RPF check in DVMRP, source verification in MOSPF
Challenge 3: Optimal routing vs scalability trade-off
Challenge 4: Heterogeneous network support - tunneling for non-multicast networks
Challenge 5: Group management - IGMP for local membership, protocol-specific for inter-domain
Challenge 6: Dynamic membership - protocols handle receiver join/leave dynamically
Comparison of Dense Mode (flood) vs Sparse Mode (explicit join) approaches

questions.question: Design a multicast routing scenario and explain how DVMRP protocol would handle it with detailed explanation of its messages and tree building process.
keyPoints: DVMRP uses Reverse Path Broadcasting for initial distribution
Prune messages remove branches without receivers
Graft messages rejoin pruned branches when new receivers join
Three phases: Initial flood, prune non-receivers, graft to rejoin
Uses distance vector approach with hop-count metric
Maintenance through periodic updates
Comparison with MOSPF link-state approach
Example topology showing source, routers, and receivers

questions.question: Compare and contrast Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) and Any-Source Multicast (ASM) models in terms of routing approaches, advantages, and limitations.
keyPoints: ASM: Receiver joins any source sending to a group; SSM: Receiver specifies source and group
ASM uses shared trees or source trees; SSM uses only source-specific trees
SSM advantages: Simpler, more secure (source known), no RP required
ASM advantages: Any source can send, more flexible
SSM limitations: Must know source address, more state per receiver
ASM issues: Shared tree bottleneck, potential source ambiguity
Protocols supporting SSM: PIM-SSM; ASM: PIM-SM, DVMRP, MOSPF
